verses.text: The LORD presses Job: shall the one who contends with the Almighty correct Him? Let the faultfinder answer. God demands a reply. To argue against God is to take a place no creature can hold.
The LORD presses Job: shall the one who contends with the Almighty correct Him? Let the faultfinder answer. God demands a reply. To argue against God is to take a place no creature can hold.

Job answers at last: 'Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth.' He is silenced and humbled. The first fruit of truly meeting God is to stop justifying ourselves.

Job answers at last: 'Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth.' He is silenced and humbled. The first fruit of truly meeting God is to stop justifying ourselves.

Job answers at last: 'Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth.' He is silenced and humbled. The first fruit of truly meeting God is to stop justifying ourselves.
Again from the whirlwind God asks: will Job annul His justice and condemn God to clear himself? He exposes the pride hidden in complaint. We must never make ourselves right by making God wrong.
Again from the whirlwind God asks: will Job annul His justice and condemn God to clear himself? He exposes the pride hidden in complaint. We must never make ourselves right by making God wrong.
Again from the whirlwind God asks: will Job annul His justice and condemn God to clear himself? He exposes the pride hidden in complaint. We must never make ourselves right by making God wrong.

Has Job an arm like God's, or a voice that thunders? Let him then array himself in majesty and pour out his wrath on the proud. The challenge is plain. Only One can govern the world, and Job is not He.

Has Job an arm like God's, or a voice that thunders? Let him then array himself in majesty and pour out his wrath on the proud. The challenge is plain. Only One can govern the world, and Job is not He.

Has Job an arm like God's, or a voice that thunders? Let him then array himself in majesty and pour out his wrath on the proud. The challenge is plain. Only One can govern the world, and Job is not He.

Let Job humble the proud and tread down the wicked everywhere; then God will own that his own hand can save him. The task is impossible to man. Salvation belongs to God alone, who needs no help to rule.

Let Job humble the proud and tread down the wicked everywhere; then God will own that his own hand can save him. The task is impossible to man. Salvation belongs to God alone, who needs no help to rule.

God points to behemoth, made alongside man, eating grass like an ox, his strength in his loins and his frame like bars of iron. The great beast is God's handiwork. If we marvel at the creature, how much more at its Maker.

God points to behemoth, made alongside man, eating grass like an ox, his strength in his loins and his frame like bars of iron. The great beast is God's handiwork. If we marvel at the creature, how much more at its Maker.
Behemoth is 'chief of the ways of God,' yet lies quietly under the shade among the reeds. Power and rest meet in him. The God who made such strength can also still it at His pleasure.
Behemoth is 'chief of the ways of God,' yet lies quietly under the shade among the reeds. Power and rest meet in him. The God who made such strength can also still it at His pleasure.
Behemoth is 'chief of the ways of God,' yet lies quietly under the shade among the reeds. Power and rest meet in him. The God who made such strength can also still it at His pleasure.
Though the river rages, behemoth is unmoved and cannot be captured by force. No man masters him. If Job cannot subdue one of God's creatures, how can he contend with God Himself?
